data.title: The UAE paid more than The Telegraph is worth. That should not be the paper’s problem to solve
data.description:
As disclosed last Friday, RedBird Capital Partners, the US private equity firm, has abandoned its bid to buy the Telegraph Media Group. We called for a new, expedited sale process to make sure that IMI, a media company owned by the United Arab Emirates, sold its call option against our company to a suitable buyer.

It has been apparent for some time that the asking price of £500m – set in order to recoup the repayment of debt owed to Lloyds Bank by the Barclay family – was too high. Most media organisations value it at around £350m.

RedBird struggled to get the deal over the line so Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, who retains a quasi-judicial role in this process should now order a speedy auction to end this limbo. Normally the paper would be sold at a market rate. But it now transpires that if the sale price fell below £500m, the Telegraph would be liable for the difference. This so-called “poison pill” was inserted into the debt repayment agreement.

However, the deal agreed between the former proprietors and IMI should not become a millstone around The Telegraph’s neck. Moreover, total transparency about what has been going on is required after so much obfuscation and backdoor dealing.

The Telegraph continues to confound those who have prematurely read the last rites over the traditional newspaper industry, producing stories that would never see the light of day on social media platforms. As we wrote last week, we need an owner who will invest in strong independent journalism and provide a strategy to grow our subscribers – and end the uncertainty that has dogged us for more than two years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: ‘Poison pill’ risks burdening Telegraph with UAE debt
data.description:
A “poison pill” threatens to burden The Telegraph with responsibility for a £500m debt to the United Arab Emirates (UAE) after a takeover bid by an American private equity firm was abandoned.

RedBird Capital Partners on Friday gave up its two-year pursuit of the newspaper, in which it cooperated with IMI, a media vehicle owned by the vice-president of the UAE.

The pair’s original bid for The Telegraph in December 2023 involved a complex deal to pay off the overdue debts of the Barclay family, the previous owners, with a new loan mostly from the Gulf state.

Now, the secret terms of that agreement threaten to load the newspaper with unsustainable debts because RedBird and IMI have failed to convert their lending into ownership.

The loan agreement valued The Telegraph at more than £500m, a price that no bidder has been able to match since.

Even RedBird, the junior partner in the original bid with continuing ties to the UAE, was unable to raise the full amount before its plans collapsed on Friday.

It is understood that the secret terms of the loan agreement mean that if it is not recouped in full in an onward sale of The Telegraph, the shortfall could land on the company’s balance sheet as additional debt.

Analysts and potential bidders have valued The Telegraph at around £350m, meaning it could face a debt to the UAE of £150m on unknown terms of interest.

The disclosure of the so-called poison pill will increase pressure on ministers to intervene to safeguard the future of The Telegraph. Sir Keir Starmer on Monday insisted he was determined to ensure a quick resolution in the public interest but the Government has refused to explain how it intends to proceed.

The conservative peer Baroness Stowell, who has played a crucial role in parliament defending The Telegraph, said: “This ‘poison pill’ which could jeopardise the Telegraph’s future is outrageous.

“We need Lisa Nandy to to come to Parliament urgently to explain what the Government is doing to get the sale process out of IMI’s control and to safeguard its future without the newspaper being burdened with the failings of money-merchants with no interest in a free press. We also need to know when Government first knew about this dangerous debt-deal.”

The poison pill has been identified by sources close to the process as a potential impediment to a sale and secure ownership.

It could mean that bidders offering a market price for The Telegraph would then be hit with a debt that would significantly curb the newspaper’s ability to invest in growth at a critical moment in the digital revolution.

The arrangement was agreed as part of a security package for IMI and RedBird as they undertook a high-risk manoeuvre in pursuit of control.

By paying off the Barclay family’s debt to Lloyds Banking Group, they derailed an auction which was being overseen by independent directors. It had strict qualification rules and a goal of finding a suitable owner as well as recouping delinquent debt.

After the attempt was blocked following a cross-party outcry over press freedom, IMI attempted to sell on its call option against The Telegraph but refused to entertain bids of less than £500m.

The only bidder who offered the sum, Dovid Efune, was subsequently unable to raise the funds. The advertising mogul Lord Saatchi led a bid of £350m plus further payments on targets being hit, but was shut out of the process after the first round.

The Telegraph is otherwise in robust financial health, according to its most recently published accounts. They showed underlying operating profits held steady at £54.6m last year, while revenues rose by 1.2pc to £279.4m.

RedBird’s founder Gerry Cardinale, who stepped in to lead a new consortium bidding £500m in May, publicly insisted The Telegraph was worth the full amount.

However, he too appeared to struggle to raise it all and backed out when the Government signalled that it would investigate the bid for the threat of foreign state influence.

A spokesman for Sir Keir Starmer said on Monday he wanted to see the sale of The Telegraph occur “as promptly as possible and in the public interest”.

Meanwhile, Dame Caroline Dinenage, chairman of the culture, media and sport committee, said The Telegraph must be protected from foreign state influence in a future sale.

“Foreign states shouldn’t have the potential to own or influence the independence of our newspapers,” the Tory MP said.

“I hope The Telegraph are able to find a new buyer that is funded by an organisation that understands the importance of maintaining a free and sustainable press.”

A spokesman for RedBird said: “We do not recognise this arrangement. Redbird is working hard to secure long-term ownership for the Telegraph.”

IMI declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: How a shoplifting epidemic is forcing supermarkets to adapt
data.description:
Jackson Mills spent large parts of his childhood in the Londis convenience store that his parents ran in Lytham St Annes, Lancashire, not far from the bright lights of Blackpool. Straight out of school, he got a job as a trainee manager at a big supermarket and didn’t look back. At 32, he is now the youngest regional manager at Co-op, where he is responsible for 120 branches across central and south London.

“Retail is in my DNA and, as my team will tell you, I’m still happiest when I’m on the shop floor,” he says, scanning the neatly stacked shelves at one of his stores near Stockwell.

Shops have always been vulnerable to thieves. Mills has had a wine bottle thrown at him during a confrontation, and once cowered with colleagues during an attempted cash machine raid. But it’s only in the past few years that crime has become so common and brazen that he can no longer remember each case.

“I’ve seen them come in with big laundry bags and just fill them up,” Mills tells me, miming the emptying of a shelf into a bag – a crime known as “sweep theft”.

“Last year we had someone going into a store with a wheelie bin. You get people stealing five or six times a day. They just don’t care, they feel immune to the law.”

Last year, thieves stole stock worth £80m from Co-op, £10m more in “shrinkage”, as it is known, than in 2023. Across the group’s more than 2,500 food stores, incidents took place at an average rate of almost 1,000 a day. Co-op tells me losses have become so great that it has considered closing some heavily targeted inner-city stores.

The numbers are bleak everywhere. Shoplifting losses hit a record £2.2bn in the year to the end of August 2024, according to the British Retail Consortium’s (BRC) latest crime survey, with more than 20 million incidents a year. Violence and abuse have also become rife, with 737,000 reported incidents in the same period, more than 25,000 of them involving weapons.

As supermarket doors buckle under an unprecedented crime wave, a feeling has percolated high streets and retail parks that retail crime has become almost unpoliceable. But Co-op has become the most vocal force in a fightback that is beginning to transform the modern supermarket.

In partnership with police, security firms, the Government, as well as suppliers of a growing array of anti-theft technology, including AI tracking and facial recognition, retailers are getting smart. But they are also having to tread carefully. How can you design shoplifting out of the aisles while respecting the privacy of your customers – and without drawing them into stores that are beginning to resemble forbidding, heavily surveilled retail fortresses?

Locked cupboards and bodycams

I am meeting Mills at Co-op’s store on the busy South Lambeth Road, next to a hairdresser’s. While Co-op won’t share branch-level crime stats, Mills tells me that this is one of its worst-hit stores. As a result, the group has chosen it as a test bed for the innovations that retailers are trialling across the country.

It starts just inside the doors, with a pair of new automatic entry and exit barriers. When a shopper comes in, they must work their way all the way round the store, via the checkout, before exiting. The system – versions of which are already common in larger supermarkets – is designed to stop opportunists who dash in from the pavement, grab whatever’s near the door and flee. Only the Wall’s ice cream freezer sits exposed, between the gates and the front doors. “We don’t get a lot of ice cream theft,” Mills says.

Inside the gates, new anti-sweep-theft innovations include secure risers on the shelves. The Perspex guards act like front-facing bookends for small products such as chocolate bars and packets of biscuits. More valuable items such as coffee are placed behind additional sliding Perspex guards that shoppers must move out of the way.

In the chillers, sea bass fillets and beef joints selling for £16.75 sit inside lock boxes with GPS trackers. They can only be opened at the checkout. Farther in, large, £14 boxes of Aptamil baby milk formula also have secure caps. The wine fridges are locked; customers must ring a bell to alert a member of staff with the key, in a kind of throwback to the counter service that pre-dated supermarkets.

On a more prominent promotions shelf near the exit, boxes containing discounted bottles of Smirnoff and Captain Morgan are empty, and must be filled behind the checkout. The kiosk itself is also fortified; steel and Perspex barriers rise to the ceiling, shielding staff from the thieves who regularly used to vault over and raid the vape and spirits shelves. Customers who want a human to check out their shopping must now pass their baskets through a hatch.

The smarter changes are more subtle. Mills guides me through the storage area, downstairs into a small office, where a packet of biscuits lies next to a redundant cash-counting machine. In the corner, screens show feeds from more than 30 CCTV cameras. With the touch of a button, staff can also activate cameras clipped to their chests.

Staff often play back footage after a theft, but nobody here is routinely watching these cameras. Instead, artificial intelligence does it remotely. The cameras are linked to a security operations centre in Northampton, where a crime intelligence team working for Mitie, the facilities and services company, is dedicated to Co-op. When a product leaves the shelf, the AI system follows it. If a bottle of wine or a pack of Pampers, say, disappears into a bag or a coat rather than being held or placed in a basket, the system alerts the Mitie team, who then check the cameras. If they determine a theft is in motion, they radio their security guards in the store.

The guards, who are dressed in Co-op uniforms, can have a quiet word with a suspected shoplifter or detain them until police arrive. “It’s absolutely incredible what it can pick up,” Mills says of the AI, which Co-op is still trialling. Mitie also gets an alert any time a Co-op employee turns on their body-worn camera. And when stores experience crime spikes, plain-clothes guards are sent to patrol the aisles, where they pretend to peruse the ready meals and confectionery.

‘Perfect storm’ of factors

Part of the response to the crime wave has been understanding who is behind it. This has long been a subject of fascination for Emmeline Taylor, a criminology professor at the University of London. She grew up in Manchester, close to a supermarket. Aged about eight, she watched a gang try to rip the safe out of the back of the store. She remembers police asking if they could play back CCTV on the family’s VHS recorder. “There was something about that moment, watching that grainy footage, that has always stayed with me,” she says.

Taylor traces the current spate of offences to the pandemic, which she says worsened and coincided with a “perfect storm” of factors, such as rising living costs, food inflation, declining mental health and underfunding in social services, policing and the courts. She also thinks psychology came into play as part of a social morality shift. She describes “entrepreneurial thieves” – people with no criminal background for whom a bit of light shoplifting and online resale feels like a slightly edgy side hustle rather than a grubby deed.

Were our role models also partly to blame? In 2022, a study published by the Barcelona School of Economics linked reported supermarket thefts to local corruption scandals. “In criminology we talk about techniques of neutralisation, or the justifications people use to convince themselves that they’re still moral while engaging in immoral behaviour,” Taylor says. “So it might be that these big companies are making record profits, or they’re replacing employees with machines… I pinpoint some of it to the pandemic lockdowns, when politicians openly lied about their conduct. People felt that their social contract with the state had broken because nobody could be relied on to be honest.”

This apparent shift also coincided with the spread of self-checkouts and their early invitation to game the system by checking out, say, expensive fruit as budget onions of a similar weight, away from human eyes. Design tweaks like those being trialled in south London may be enough to nudge these thieves back on to a moral course. The latest self-checkouts, for example, include cameras, smarter scales and receipt scanners at exit gates in larger stores.

But it also became clear to Taylor and the big retailers that the crime wave was too big simply to be the result of opportunism. Organised criminals were getting in on the game in a big way, recruiting vulnerable or drug-habituated people, often on day rates, and sending them out with shopping lists. “These are what we call poly-criminals, who just do whatever is most profitable and lowest risk at any given time,” Taylor says.

The criminals sell the loot on, at low prices, to complicit corner shops, pubs and cafes – or online. Suddenly, even low-value items such as butter have become worth stealing at volume. The organised element has also brought with it more violence, especially when criminals were apprehended. Yet typically crimes would not be reported, or suspects would be allowed to flee because stores knew that police were unlikely to show up for what were, in isolation, low-value thefts.

Taylor estimates that, last year, fewer than 3 per cent of all shoplifting incidents known to retailers were reported to police. And according to the BRC’s crime survey, retailers reported fewer than a third of incidents of violence and abuse. Only 10 per cent of these incidents resulted in police attendance.

As well as a growing sense of lawlessness, I am told there was also a feeling among some retailers that it was better to let thieves go than to cause a scene or risk staff safety. Crime is bad for business in several ways: who wants to shop at stores with a reputation as perennial crime scenes? Certainly there is a widespread reluctance even to talk publicly about the problem; of the 10 major chains I contacted, only Co-op agreed to engage meaningfully.

Owned by its members, Co-op has also been vocal in Westminster, where the government has now woken up to the scale and nature of the problem. In 2023, it announced a new retail crime action plan, which included a commitment by police to attend stores when violence had been reported or a suspect had been detained, and to investigate prolific offenders. It also established Operation Pegasus, a retailer-funded police intelligence unit at Opal, a national intelligence unit for serious organised acquisitive crime (SOAC).

The Crime and Policing Bill now going through Parliament is also expected to drop a £200-value threshold that has blocked a proper response to “low-level” theft. The new law would also introduce a specific offence of assaulting a retail worker. Meanwhile, retailers are being encouraged to report all crime.

Targeting prolific offenders

Newly motivated police forces have started to join the dots. In Bromley in south London, Det Supt Luke Baldock has led an operation to gather evidence of prolific offenders in partnership with stores. His team reviewed CCTV footage, and soon recognised patterns of behaviour and familiar faces. Ultimately he established that just 13 criminals were responsible for around 80 per cent of thefts in the area. Arrests soon followed.

Last year, the National Business Crime Solution (NBCS), a non-profit organisation that targets business crime alongside police forces, exposed a group of Romanian criminals who would communicate via Bluetooth earpieces in supermarkets and hit emergency alarms before fleeing with sparkling wine. The “champagne gang” pulled off 60 hits across England over a year, pinching fizz worth at least £73,000.

Police have also got involved in preventative operations. Earlier this year, in a separate partnership with Co-op, Metropolitan Police officers in plain clothes hid in the backroom of a store near Waterloo railway station, waiting for a repeat offender to return. When he appeared on CCTV, officers swooped, tackling him as he began raiding the meat chiller.

“If you look at shoplifting as a macro issue, it almost looks impossible to solve,” Baldock tells me. “But when you start dealing with someone stealing a few Mars bars, you find out they’ve committed 20 other crimes and all of a sudden you’re charging them with £10,000-worth of shoplifting over the space of a couple of months.” In parts of Bromley, retail crime dropped by about 70 per cent following the operation.

Baldock covers a small patch of south London. More widely, police forces still face an uphill struggle. Across the capital, the Met tells me there were almost 50,000 shoplifting offences in the year to October, a slight rise on the previous 12 months. Increased reporting may partly explain this. There was also an increase in cases resulting in a charge, from 5 to 11 per cent year on year. The BRC is due to publish its next national crime survey early in 2026.

Baldock wants police to keep up the pressure. He has come to see the targeting of shoplifters in spaces where good CCTV evidence is relatively easy to gather as a strategic opportunity. “There’s this concept that a shoplifter is a burglar on his day off,” he says. “By dealing with them for this, you’re going to stop them breaking into a car or house or worse. It’s a win-win for communities and for controlling some of the most harmful people that we have.”

Killing criminals with kindness

In two stores in London and Bath, Sainsbury’s is trialling live facial recognition, perhaps the most controversial anti-shoplifting measure. The system, supplied by a firm called Facewatch, includes cameras at the entrance that instantly compare customers’ faces with a database of faces that stores can upload themselves when they check existing footage of shoplifters. A match creates an alert.

“We’ve got a tablet by the till that will make a sound,” says Nathalie Fullerton, who runs an independent convenience store in Glasgow. In the year that she has been operating Facewatch, she has added 31 faces to her database and prevented an estimated £8,000 of theft. “Quite a few times, the person will hear it and just go, ‘Oh, s---’, and walk back out again.”

If a flagged suspect carries on into the store, Fullerton will “kill them with kindness. We’ll say, ‘Are you all right there? Are you looking for something in particular?’ Or if they’re holding a couple of items, we’ll politely offer them a basket. When they know we’re watching, they’ll usually put their items down and leave.”

In perhaps an unlikely origin story for an AI tech firm, Facewatch was born at Gordon’s Wine Bar, a central London drinking hole. The owner, Simon Gordon, had grown tired of pickpockets and set up his own CCTV system more than a decade ago. The firm is now an international player in retail security and already operates in Home Bargains, Costcutter and Spar stores.

But its tech has also prompted questions about the tightening web of high street surveillance. The privacy group Big Brother Watch has described it as “Orwellian” and “deeply disproportionate”. Last year, a woman told BBC News that she had been thrown out of a store after a bag search and told she had been banned from the chain when Facewatch wrongly flagged her as a suspect.

A senior figure in retail security tells me that many big players are holding off on the tech as a result of what they see as its untested legal status. But Nick Fisher, a former retail executive and Facewatch’s chief executive, is bullish about the firm’s approach to privacy. He says biometric data from unmatched faces is instantly deleted and that faces are only added to the database locally, unless the company determines that a record of repeat or violent offences justifies flagging suspects in stores in a wider area or nationally.

Fisher says Facewatch analysts review CCTV footage and reports submitted by a store before a manager is allowed to upload any new face to the database, and that its technology has been reviewed by the the Information Commissioner’s Office, the government body that upholds information rights. He says misidentifications are rare. “This year, we’ll send 600,000 alerts, and you can count on one hand how many times we’ve got it wrong,” Fisher says.

He thinks civil liberties groups are “alarmist and inflammatory”, and says that the tech is more consistent than letting security guards recognise and potentially turn away suspected shoplifters. “Retailers have a right to protect their assets and their people,” he adds. “And all the statistics tell you that this thing’s totally out of control.”

On its website, Sainsbury’s says the system is clearly signposted, adding: “We believe that everyone deserves to feel safe when they come to work or to shop with us… Privacy is at the heart of the [Facewatch] trial, with all data handled in line with UK law.”

Emmeline Taylor sees the spread of live facial recognition and other smart, invisible CCTV systems as inevitable, as stores try to balance the “fortress” approach, which, she predicts, risks pushing more customers to shopping online. She hopes that legislation catches up with the privacy implications of the tech and its potential future applications. Though not currently a feature, it is not difficult to imagine future AI CCTV systems being used to analyse the age or mood of customers, say, and marketing products accordingly. “Surveillance can be a slippery slope,” Taylor adds.

A turning of the tide?

Co-op tells me it has no plans to trial live facial recognition. It may not need to; it says that the effects of its own crackdown, along with the improved response rate and engagement from police, is starting to show an effect, after more than £240m of investment over five years. Kiosk breaches are down by 26 per cent, year on year; physical assaults have dropped by 32 per cent; and antisocial behaviour and verbal abuse are down 30 per cent. Overall, crimes have dropped by 15 per cent and there are signs that the total value of stolen goods may no longer be rising.

In south London, Jackson Mills says regular customers have adjusted to the new measures. “When the fortified kiosks went in, they said, ‘God, what’s this?’, but I think people understand because they see shoplifting themselves and it upsets them too.” He says staff appreciate the reinforced checkouts in particular. “I used to be scared to be here, but now it feels like I’m in a different shop,” says Atish Patel, the store manager.

It might feel slightly optimistic, but Mills sees stores – especially smaller outlets like the one he grew up in – as a vital realm at the junction of commerce and community. The crime wave has threatened not just his stock and staff, but that space. He is pleased that Co-op is tackling the problem so openly. “My customers need to know what’s happening, but also that, while there’s still more to do, things are getting better,” he says, surveying the shelves again. “The tide is turning.”

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Starmer’s nuclear revolution is about PowerPoints, not power
data.description:
Another week and another lie from a government that only seems capable of pulling defeat from the jaws of victory.

Last week found Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, trumpeting “the largest nuclear building programme in Britain in half a century”.

But at the same time, the Government is making promises to the people of North Wales – and to the rest of the UK – that it can’t possibly fulfil. It will do nothing to help us keep the lights on in 2030, after most of our existing nuclear fleet has been shut down.

“This isn’t ‘going big’ – it’s going small as slowly as possible,” one industry source told me.

“It’s PowerPoints, not power,” one source fretted.

The Government has approved three new small modular nuclear reactors at the Wylfa site on Anglesey. The implication is that spades will be in the ground very soon. But by choosing Rolls-Royce SMR, that cannot possibly happen.

Rolls-Royce is a great British engine company and the decision makes sense if you are not in a hurry, and want to use the state to rebuild onshore manufacturing capacity.

But Rolls-Royce was very late to the race and is trailing in the pack. Perhaps this will come as a surprise to some. But some of the coverage has been highly misleading.

Like this example: “Rolls-Royce is already world-leading when it comes to making small modular reactors. It is just the ones they make currently go into submarines,” Sam Dumitriu of the think tank Britain Remade told readers of The Spectator last month. He’s the magazine’s go-to nuclear expert.

Not only does Rolls-Royce not lead the world, being one of the last to start its design process, but comparing military and civilian reactors is like saying a horse is a slightly larger goldfish. They are completely different creatures, adapted to different habitats.

Submarine reactors are boutique military engines which use highly enriched uranium. Most are never refuelled.

The safety case for a military reactor is simple: get everyone out and sink the boat. In civilian nuclear energy, the safety case must be much more rigorous to win public trust, more reliable and with extensive redundant safety systems.

Rolls-Royce recognised all this when it teamed up with the SMR specialist and pioneer NuScale in 2013. NuScale would design the reactors and shepherd them through the regulatory process, while Rolls-Royce would then bid to build NuScale’s designs.

When that relationship ended, Rolls-Royce had to start from scratch with a fairly generic pressurised water reactor (PWR) design of its own. At that point, over a decade had elapsed since NuScale submitted its first design in 2008.

A year ago, Miliband ejected NuScale from the UK’s SMR competition because it was mature and didn’t need subsidies. Make of that what you will.

Creating a detailed design case takes years and Rolls-Royce is at stage three of the Generic Design Assessment (GDA) process. Then a safety case must be assembled.

Korea’s reactors took almost two decades to get from submission to construction. Rolls-Royce SMR set sail in November 2021. Perhaps it can be done in a decade. Yet the Government has promised 3,000 jobs in North Wales and 5,000 across the UK, and the Welsh are looking for a boost to the economy sooner rather than later.

So if spades are going into the ground early next year, what exactly will they be digging? Holes at random? Apparently so.

“If you put spades in the ground before your design is finished, you are really baking in a recipe for a catastrophe. You’ll be digging a hole on one place, filling it in and digging another 250 metres to the east,” says one source.

Wonks like Dumitriu position themselves as nuclear advocates who want it built quicker, but as one nuclear expert told me in exasperation last week: “It’s a terrible decision to choose a technology they have not finished designing yet. That rushes the design and that is the number one reason for cost overruns.”

We wouldn’t have to rush if we chose a mature, approved design.

In addition, Wylfa is the only site in the UK that is currently licenced to accommodate a big, gigawatt-scale reactor generating as much power as three SMR tiddlers.

“Wylfa was our best site for our next gigawatt nuclear plant, which is why I signed one off there,” Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, told me. “It’s big enough to do both small and large nuclear. It would be a huge downgrade of ambition to only do small nuclear reactors there.”

The previous government recognised the two conflicting goals with its twin-track SMR competition: build a homegrown candidate slowly and also get new nuclear capacity on to the grid quickly. Sir Keir Starmer’s Government has fudged it.

No wonder Warren Stephens, the American ambassador to the UK, was fuming last week. He had pitched a mature and fully approved AP1000 for Wylfa, a design that we can speed through our equivalent regulatory process.

But we should be even angrier than Stephens – by 2030, he’ll be safely back in an energy-secure and prospering United States. It’s our lights that will be going out.

data.title: Nobody’s buying London’s shiny new homes
data.description:
They are supposed to be the solution to London’s housing crisis – but new-build properties in the capital are increasingly unloved by buyers.

Construction of new homes in the capital has ground to a halt and the situation looks unlikely to change.

Developers are abandoning building sites and just 6pc of the 176,000 homes promised by Sir Sadiq Khan in the next two years are likely to be built, according to consultancy Molior.

Everything from onerous red tape to Sir Sadiq’s mandate that over a third of new housing be sold at a discount has been blamed for the slowdown.

But one factor stifling the city’s supply of new-build homes has arguably been overlooked: almost nobody is buying them.

The latest available figures for the first six months of the year show that only 3,946 new private housing units changed hands in London, down 30pc compared with the same period in 2024, and the lowest level since the global financial crisis.

The decline is far more drastic when compared with sales of housing overall in the city, which dropped by about 1pc compared with last year, analysis by estate agents Hamptons shows.

Housing has become unaffordable

One big factor behind the market slump is the change to property tax.

Would-be homeowners in the capital were hit hard when the tax-free stamp duty allowance was halved in April from £250,000 to £125,000. In addition, the nil rate threshold for first-time buyers fell from £425,000 to £300,000, costing them an additional £7,000 on average.

Sales of new-build property in particular saw a significant hit a month later. Just 19 new flat sales were processed across the whole city in May, according to data from the Land Registry.

The figures are likely to be revised upwards in the coming years, but suggest transactions of new housing in the capital have declined significantly.

As London developers struggle to shift new housing stock in the face of weak demand, plans to build thousands more new properties have grown uncertain amid fears they simply will not sell.

Neal Hudson, of housing market research firm Built Place, explained how Britain’s acute housing shortage has failed to produce an excess of eager buyers in London.

“You need to sell flats upfront to unlock the finance to get them built,” he says. Without this, developers face the risk that buyers who are willing to pay a premium for a new home won’t materialise, and so being left with unsold stock.

Buyer sentiment has also been affected by higher interest rates in the aftermath of Liz Truss’s mini-Budget in 2022 which has made mortgages unaffordable, Hudson says.

If an average worker in London wants to buy a home, the average purchase price is more than 13 times their salary, compared with eight times the average UK salary for homes across England, Office for National Statistics (ONS) data shows.

Even high earners in the capital face an uphill battle to afford to buy. The highest 10pc of earners (those with salaries of more than £90,000 a year) will be paying six times their salary, on average, to afford property in London.

To buy in London, first-time buyers now need to save £124,688 on average for a deposit based on a £511,514 average property price, according to data from Halifax. This is far higher than the national average deposit of £61,090 for the average house price of £311,034.

Saving for a house while renting is also increasingly difficult. Average rents in the capital have been rising for years and now stand at £2,121 per month, according to Zoopla.

Figures from the ONS showed that last year, Londoners earning the median salary were spending more than 40pc of their salary on rent, reducing their ability to put money aside.

New-builds are pushed further out of the reach of buyers because they tend to be more expensive than older, equivalent properties.

Newly finished properties in London come with a price premium of £3,300 per square meter when compared with older properties, according to research published earlier this year by property market analysts Investropa.

Hudson says new housing developments in London have all but “disappeared” as a result. The number of new building starts in London has fallen by over 70pc in the past year, according to the Centre for Policy Studies, a think tank.

Historically, developers were geared towards a healthy flow of foreign buyers, says Hudson, but these buyers are also turning away from the capital. “Particularly in inner London, [new properties] have been focused on overseas investors for the past 20 years,” he says.

Data from earlier this year released by Hamptons showed foreign buyers were just 3pc of all house hunters in the first three months of 2025, down from 4pc last year and a peak of 8pc in 2009.

Hudson adds: “Fundamentally it comes down to price and type of homes that are being delivered [...] It’s a product and a price point that is simply not attractive or affordable to the vast majority of London residents.”

There are no quick fixes to address the problem, he concludes. “Over the longer term things like planning reform will work but in the short term things are difficult. We’ve already seen flexibility around the affordable housing provision and it’s looking likely at some point we’ll see a new version of Help to Buy.”

A riskier investment

One of the motivations for buying your own home is the hope that its value will increase, leaving you better off and making it easier for you to move up the ladder when you come to sell.

However, for new-builds this is far from guaranteed. Those who choose to buy a new-build in London have often faced stunted house price growth, in part due to high service charge fees attached to their property.

Average values for all kinds of new-builds, including flats and houses, grew by 3.1pc last year. This was less than half of the house price growth enjoyed by owners of older properties during that time, according to data from Hamptons.

New-build flats had particularly weak price growth at just under 3pc, compared with 5.2pc for older flats.

Buyers of new-builds are also more likely to make a loss when it comes to selling their home in the capital, the same figures reveal.

Among sellers who lost money in London last year, one third were owners of new-build flats. Overall, 9pc of those making a loss sold older properties while 30pc were selling new-builds.

Added to this is the fact that almost all new developments are sold on a leasehold basis and come with a yearly service charge that is supposed to pay for amenities and basic maintenance of shared areas in residential buildings.

But recent research by the Greater London Authority (GLA) found leaseholders were spending on average £3,912 a year, or £326 a month, on service charges. One in 10 surveyed by the GLA were spending more than £7,000 per year, reducing their resell value and in some instances making them un-mortgageable.

The GLA report also highlighted complaints of poor transparency in new shared-ownership schemes, where residents pay rent and a mortgage for the right to resell a fraction of a property, over fees charged for maintenance that is poorly done or left incomplete.

Separate research by Hamptons last year showed that the average service charge for a flat in London was £2,633, up 40pc in five years.

David Fell, an analyst at Hamptons, said these charges were increasingly being avoided by buyers in the city.

“A lot of the new-build schemes [have] lots of amenities and you are paying a service charge for more amenities, but the cost of running those has risen compared with a Victorian house that’s been split into two or three. Buyers don’t want to open themselves up to paying for all of those things,” says Fell.

This has had a marked impact at the top end of London’s housing market, where new luxury housing developments also come with yearly service charges for residents that are often justified by a wide array of on-site leisure facilities and management staff.

Mark Pollack, of London-based estate agents Aston Chase, says buyers are being put off by uncapped monthly charges when purchasing property. “People are more forensic about service charges when they look into a building than they used to be. Developers have seen management alongside a new block of flats as an opportunity to extract further money [and] leaseholders want far more accountability now.”

Red tape is blocking building

Under mounting pressure to resuscitate housebuilding in London, last week Sir Sadiq dropped his affordable housing quota for new developments from 35pc to 20pc in an effort to make the city more attractive to developers.

The London Mayor set a target to build 88,000 new homes in the capital every year. But he also set strict rules for developers to discount properties, initially planning for half of all new homes to be sold below market value.

The policy has been blamed for making building in London difficult and unaffordable, as have new rules for building residential blocks set by the Building Safety Regulator (BSR), a quango established in the aftermath of the Grenfell Tower fire.

The BSR applies some of the strictest rules to residential blocks that are over 18m tall or with seven or more storeys, a form of development that is common in London. These buildings are required to go through a three-stage approvals process during planning and construction.

data.title: UAE claims Telegraph won’t face ‘poison pill’ debt
data.description:
The UAE has claimed a “poison pill” debt that threatened to derail an onward sale of The Telegraph will not apply.

RedBird IMI signalled on Tuesday that The Telegraph will not bear responsibility for any outstanding debts owed by the Barclay family, its former owners, who had used the newspaper as collateral.

The joint venture, which is majority funded by the United Arab Emirates, said it expected the Telegraph’s liabilities to be “fully extinguished and discharged” following any onward sale.

The statement is intended to ease concerns the paper faced unsustainable debts from the outstanding loan to the Barclays and will remove a potential block for any future buyer.

A RedBird IMI spokesman said on Tuesday: “The current security over The Telegraph companies secures the loan owed to Redbird IMI by the Barclay family, and a loan owed to Lloyds by The Telegraph.

“The structure of the transaction has always been that upon any sale, the security and guarantees granted by The Telegraph companies in respect of the RedBird IMI loan will be fully extinguished and discharged. Further, The Telegraph would not assume any debt owed by the Barclay family.

“RedBird IMI is working hard to progress a sale and secure long-term ownership for the Telegraph.”

Details of the potential poison pill were revealed on Monday. Under a complex arrangement struck between RedBird IMI and the Barclay family, The Telegraph could have been liable for a £500m loan if the Barclays were unable to pay.

Documents show that the Government signed off on the arrangement following a request from RedBird IMI as it sought ownership of The Telegraph.

In a letter to the Abu Dhabi-based fund in April last year, the Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) granted RedBird IMI’s request to tie The Telegraph to the debt.

A second letter in July confirmed that Lucy Frazer, culture secretary at the time, consented to the debts being transferred to a new holding company under a so-called “hive down” restructuring arrangement.

Ministers acknowledged The Telegraph would be liable for the loan in the event that the UAE-backed takeover collapsed. That deal was subsequently blocked on press freedom grounds.

The arrangement threatened to saddle The Telegraph with unsustainable levels of debt and torpedo any onward sale after RedBird Capital Partners, a junior partner in RedBird IMI, alongside International Media Investments (IMI), withdrew a separate bid for the paper last week.

Speaking in New York on Tuesday, Gerry Cardinale, managing partner of RedBird Capital Partners, said he had dropped the bid because it “got too political, too politicised, and we really just couldn’t get any transparency on the regulatory approval process”.

Mr Cardinale added that RedBird would “find the right owner… given the current circumstance”, suggesting he hopes to play a role in a future auction of The Telegraph.

RedBird IMI’s new guarantee will raise hopes of ending the near three years of uncertainty for The Telegraph, with calls for ministers to facilitate a swift onward sale at a fair price.

The loan agreement has valued The Telegraph at more than £500m – a price that no bidder has been willing to match.

Peers are on Wednesday set to demand that Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, hands over control of the auction of The Telegraph to an independent body.

Lord Fox, who has spearheaded efforts to limit foreign state ownership of newspapers, warned that the Culture Secretary “cannot be trusted” to oversee the sale.

He said she had “totally failed to read the financial room”, adding that her department’s actions have “worsened the Telegraph’s situation”.

The Liberal Democrat peer will use a parliamentary question to pressure ministers on how they plan to end the impasse over The Telegraph’s ownership.

It is understood that The Telegraph’s board of directors, including Mike McTighe, Stephen Welch and Boudewijn Wentink, approved the loan arrangement between RedBird IMI and the Barclays – unaware that it could disrupt any future sale process.

A source said a similar arrangement had also been in place for a loan between Barclays and Lloyds Banking Group.

RedBird IMI said last year it would secure an onward sale of its position but no deal has been agreed a year on.

Attempts by RedBird to mount a predominately US-led bid were abandoned last week after the spectre of a parliamentary investigation was raised.

Analysts and potential acquirers have valued The Telegraph at around £350m, raising fears that the newspaper could face a debt to the UAE of £150m under the poison pill arrangement.

It is understood that if The Telegraph sells for less than £500m, no debt will fall on the paper or its subsidiaries.

Lord Fox told The Telegraph Ms Nandy should give up control of the sale process.

He said: “When Lisa Nandy hastily tabled the legislation aimed at allowing this misguided RedBird deal to succeed, it was clearly not right.

“Her department’s failure to read the financial room has only worsened The Telegraph’s situation. Those currently involved cannot be trusted to do the right thing.”

Lord Fox added: “Given its performance so far, DCMS cannot be trusted to ask the right questions and make the right decisions. The sale must be driven independently or at the very least by the Cabinet Office.”

DCMS declined to comment.

data.title: Starmer pledges to spend billions more on drugs in NHS overhaul
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer is preparing to boost NHS spending on drugs to prevent an exodus of investment from the UK.

The Government is set to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. The move will allow the NHS to pay billions of pounds more for medicines.

Several drug companies have scrapped investments in the UK amid a row over how much the Government was spending on new drugs compared to other countries.

The pricing shake-up will appease drugmakers – but it also threatens to put Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, on a collision course with Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, over how to fund the multibillion-pound bill.

Lord Vallance, the science minister – who is negotiating a new deal with the industry on behalf of the Government – admitted last month that price increases were necessary to stop a wave of investment leaving the UK.

The Times reported that the Treasury is insisting that spending increases must be funded through existing departmental budgets, while NHS bosses are demanding extra financial support to pay for any deal.

The looming showdown comes as the Chancellor prepares to unveil another tax-raising Budget, with economists predicting extra levies of around £30bn to balance the books.

Ms Reeves boosted health spending at the expense of other government departments in her June spending review, with the department accounting for 90pc of the extra money for public services over the next three years.

It means the NHS and other health spending is now on course to account for half of all spending on public services by the end of the decade.

Meanwhile, a string of companies including Merck, the US cancer drug manufacturer, and Eli Lily, maker of obesity jab Mounjaro, have halted investment in the UK over the past year in protest against the NHS’s drug pricing regime.

This week, AstraZeneca’s UK chairman signalled that the company was ready to scrap investment in Britain unless the Government backed down on pricing.

Shaun Grady warned that the UK was in danger of becoming unattractive for companies unless it brings its “appalling” drugs regime into the 21st century.

Pharmaceutical companies are also under pressure from Donald Trump, the US president, who has escalated tensions by threatening 100pc tariffs on the sector if it does not invest in America.

The antiquated system used by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) to determine which treatments are available on the NHS has not been changed since 1999.

The system uses a formula which seeks to determine value for money based on a drug’s effectiveness.

Currently, Nice will approve medicines that cost less than £20,000-£30,000 for every extra “quality-adjusted life year”.

A new range of between £25,000-£35,000 per year is said to be one option under consideration.

However, the Association of the British Pharmaceutical Industry has warned that the threshold would be closer to £56,000 if it had climbed in line with the cost of living. It has called for a range closer to £50,000.

A government spokesman said: “The pharmaceutical sector and the innovative medicines it produces are critical to our NHS, our economy and the plan for change.

“Through our life sciences sector plan, we’ve committed to working with industry to accelerate growth in spending on innovative medicines compared to the previous decade.

“We’ve secured a landmark economic partnership with the US that includes working together on pharmaceutical exports from the UK whilst improving conditions for pharmaceutical companies here.

“We’re now in advanced discussions with the US administration to secure the best outcome for the UK, reflecting our strong relationship and the opportunities from close partnership with our pharmaceutical industry.”

data.title: Labour net zero levies now main driver of rising energy bills
data.description:
Labour’s net zero levies will become the main driver of household energy bills next year, overtaking gas prices for the first time, analysts have warned.

Green taxes, upgrades to the electricity grid and other government-backed charges will account for 60pc of energy bills from April 2026, Cornwall Insight said – far outweighing the actual cost of energy.

The shift undermines claims by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, that gas prices are the primary cause of high bills. He has previously said that bills were high “because of our dependence on fossil fuels”.

Craig Lowrey, from Cornwall Insight, said: “Wholesale prices are no longer the main story. The real pressure is coming from rising non-energy costs, with levies and policy decisions associated with that investment in renewables driving up bills.”

Household energy bills will surge to more than £1,800 next spring, Cornwall Insight predicted, partly because of the cost of upgrading the UK’s pylons, power cables and substations.

Average household power and gas bills, currently standing at £1,755, will dip by £22 in January only to surge back to £1,808 from April, it warned.

“This [increase] is largely due to rising charges associated with the operation and maintenance of the country’s energy networks, specifically electricity transmission and gas distribution charges,” said Cornwall Insight.

“This is likely to be an enduring trend with wholesale energy prices, once the dominant driver of bills, forecast to drop to less than 40pc of the cap and remain below that threshold for the rest of the decade.”

Cornwall Insight’s forecast includes a new levy averaging £10 per household per year to subsidise the construction of the Sizewell C nuclear power station in Suffolk.

The total predicted price rises add to the pre-Budget pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to tackle the surging costs of Mr Miliband’s net zero crusade.

For Labour, there is also growing concern that the Energy Secretary’s net zero price rises will hit energy bills just weeks before May’s elections covering the Scottish and Welsh governments and many English local authorities.

The Conservatives and Reform have both pledged to put energy costs at the heart of their campaigns.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “Ed Miliband promised to cut bills by £300 but struggling households are set to face yet another bill rise next year. It’s now patently clear: Labour’s energy policies can’t cut bills.”

The run-up to the Budget has included suggestions that Ms Reeves would slash energy bills by £200, including removing the 5pc VAT added to power and gas bills.

The UK has had some of the world’s highest energy costs ever since the sharp rise caused by the 2022 energy crisis. Average bills rose from £1,179 in the autumn of 2019 to £1,755 today.

While the initial surge was driven by a spike in gas prices, costs are now being kept high mainly by the 11 or so levies and other policy costs used to fund the expansion of renewables.

Household energy debts have already surged from £2bn three years ago to £4.4bn this summer, with energy consultancy Baringa warning they will approach £6bn in 2026.

Baringa, which has access to the debt books of major suppliers, suggests a quarter of UK households will struggle to pay their gas and electricity bills this coming winter.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “We know energy bills are still too high, which is why the Government is taking urgent action to support families this winter, including expanding the £150 Warm Home Discount to 2.7 million more households.

“The only way to bring down energy bills for good is with our clean energy superpower mission, which will get the UK off the roller-coaster of fossil fuel prices and onto clean, home-grown power that we control.”

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: ‘It has a dystopian feeling’: The residents still waiting for the town they were promised
data.description:
After swapping London for a new life in Sherford, Jack Dowson noticed something peculiar. “There was nothing,” he says. “When I moved here, there wasn’t even a convenience store.”

Dowson was drawn to the south Devon town by the developers’ artistic impressions, which painted a picture of a vibrant community and a buzzy town centre.

But several months later, he says the reality of life has set in. “It has a very dystopian feeling. Nobody really gets out and does things together,” he says. “You need a car to be able to get anywhere.”

The initial plans for Sherford envisioned a walkable market town comprising 5,500 homes, shops, restaurants, schools and leisure facilities that would “become a model for town planning in the 21st century”.

But a decade on, its 3,500 residents are served by just one temporary cafe and an adjoining container-like storage unit offering essentials such as tea and biscuits, which is kept locked up unless someone wants to buy something.

Sherford is far from the only new development to adopt a houses first, amenities later approach.

Fifty minutes up the road, Cranbrook, also in Devon, and Northstowe in Cambridgeshire, initially came under scrutiny as homes sprang up while the delivery of shops and restaurants lagged well behind. Both have recently added amenities, and developers insist they are “committed to creating thriving communities”.

As Labour races to roll out its new town plans and meet its ambitious target of building 1.5 million homes by 2029, Sherford may serve as a warning.

‘A betrayal’

Poundbury, a town on the outskirts of Dorchester, was the inspiration for Sherford. Endorsed by then-Prince Charles, Poundbury bucked post-war town-planning trends by prioritising pedestrians over cars, integrating businesses with private and social housing and adhering to traditional regional architectural styles.

But after a consortium of commercial developers, which bought the project in 2014, successfully won approval to water down the prescriptive town code, critics claim the civic needs of the local population fell down the list of priorities.

Ben Bolgar, of The Prince’s Foundation (now called The King’s Foundation), the master planner for Sherford, branded the move a betrayal, adding that it had put “the wishes of the volume housebuilders ahead of the wishes of the local people”.

Today, Sherford’s nearest supermarket is a 15-minute drive along a busy main road, while most have no choice but to make the five-mile trip to Plymouth for the most basic services. The temporary community hub, also housed in a container-like structure, serves as a base for all types of meetups, from religious congregations to Girl Scout groups.

“There’s no GP surgery,” says Cat Burchell, a resident. “It’s a pain.”

As frustration grows at the sclerotic delivery of amenities, the project’s developers – Taylor Wimpey and Vistry Group – have sought to assuage concerns. In August, they said they had finally applied for a town centre, including a 4,500 sq ft convenience store, with a completion date some time in 2027.

But for those who have waited years for the bustling community they assumed would be here by now, fresh promises only highlight what they still don’t have. “It’s not just about shops. They mis-sold us on everything – an entire lifestyle that was a lie,” says Eleanor Faith, who arrived in 2022. “I truly feel I have been conned.”

‘Zombie town’

South Hams District Council commissioned The Prince’s Foundation to create a concept for Sherford in 2004. But in 2017, under pressure to meet government-mandated housing targets, South Hams granted developers in charge of the project permission to relax design restrictions.

This was despite objections from Plymouth City Council, which warned that diluting the original plans risked creating a “zombie town”.

South Hams councillor David Hancock says “the application was considered on its merits” and “allowed more flexibility to enable the scheme to adapt and evolve,” rather than reduce the level of amenities.

In the name of accelerating development, the changes included a more flexible approach to house design, more visible car parking and alterations to how streets were laid out.

Walking around Sherford, it is clear to see the physical impact of these changes to the code.

In the area encompassing the first phase of development, wide pavements are lined with handsome Georgian-style townhouses, and there are sports and children’s play facilities. On the other, less developed side, houses still under construction have a more uniform feel, with no facilities in sight.

Steve Fowler, Sherford’s project director, says that, given a project of Sherford’s timescale, the original plan would inevitably have to “adapt and evolve” over time, and says the consortium’s intention is never to mislead anyone.

The Sherford Consortium adds that it “has always been keen to show the vision for what Sherford will become – as well as being honest and transparent about what homebuyers can expect”.

It says it is “still delivering the same community facilities, new homes, and open space. The civic needs of local people remain central to delivery, as evidenced by the facilities already delivered and those in the pipeline.”

Yet with none of the key tenets required to form a strong community – or even the structures in place to house them – locals have been left feeling isolated.

“With a lack of bars and coffee shops, it just seems like everyone is apart from each other,” says Dowson. “I think people are sort of socially isolated. Everything is segmented. There’s the occasional get-together, but there is no real socialisation of the people here.”

A chicken-and-egg problem

Not everyone feels so aggrieved. Joanne Hilton, who runs Sherford’s temporary cafe, says she’s seen a growing sense of community since moving to the town in 2018. “I absolutely love it here. I wouldn’t be anywhere else,” she says. “If anything is going on, people seem to come together and help each other out.

“With any new build, all those things come later. I didn’t move here for shops, but I know all of that will come.”

Fowler blames the slow delivery of amenities on the project’s “complexity”. Difficulties in gaining commitments for sites when the town is still under construction have also been a stumbling block. For example, a temporary space will be provided for a GP surgery. Still, it will ultimately be the NHS’s responsibility to fund the rest and find doctors willing to work there.

Similarly, many retailers are often hesitant to set up shop in areas until certain occupation thresholds are met. This can create a chicken-and-egg situation, where people may be reluctant to move to an area because of a lack of amenities, but the amenities will only appear when there are enough people.

As Sherford moves ever closer to its target of 12,000 residents, arguments around thresholds are inevitably losing weight. Some of those who have lived in the town for years can no longer fathom why delivery has been so slow. For young families, the lack of a promised secondary school and youth club is proving particularly painful.

For lack of anything to do, anti-social behaviour among young people has reportedly become a problem.

As a result, some residents say their kids have been forced to avoid the few facilities that do exist.

‘You can’t just keep building houses’

Adding to the frustrations of Sherford’s shop-less residents is news of success stories elsewhere.

Nansledan, in Cornwall, has nearly 900 homes and more than 40 local businesses 12 years after work on the project began. The new suburb, which received the Duchy of Cornwall’s endorsement, was designed to relieve pressure on nearby Newquay and subscribed to the same principles as Poundbury, including walkable neighbourhoods and mixed-use buildings.

Meon Vale, near Stratford-upon-Avon, which comprises around 1,000 new homes, also has restaurants, a shop, a community centre and a business park that appeared simultaneously. “The town hall, shop and leisure centre were some of the first things to be built,” says Faith, who used to live in Meon Vale. “That is the way it used to be.”

Rebecca Smith, Conservative MP for South West Devon, says: “We face a significant challenge with the delivery of amenities in new towns and new housing developments in general. It is not unique to Sherford.”

For those who have laid roots in the town, however, all they can do now is wait. “I do like living here,” Burchell says. “But you can just see they’re building, building, building. You can’t just keep building houses. You need infrastructure in this town.”
