data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The BBC is losing British kids to YouTube. Our culture could pay the price
data.description:
It may come as a surprise that one of the BBC’s biggest money-spinners is an animated puppy called Bluey.

The British broadcaster owns the global distribution and merchandising rights to the children’s TV series, which was the most-streamed show in America in 2024.

It is now also teaming up with Disney to make a film expected to be released in 2027.

Yet Bluey is an outlier, sitting alongside Peppa Pig in a rarefied world of global children’s TV successes.

Nor will it have escaped the attention of parents that one of the BBC’s biggest hits is, in fact, Australian.

In reality, the BBC – and British broadcasters more widely – are facing a crisis when it comes to children’s television.

An exodus of young audiences to YouTube means viewing of children’s TV on traditional channels has plummeted.

In turn, advertising revenues have dwindled and the industry has slashed its investment in the genre.

This, broadcasters warn, could have deeply damaging effects as children are increasingly pushed to programming from the US and other parts of the world.

TV bosses fear that without government intervention, British culture could disappear from young people’s screens altogether.

At the heart of a crisis is an exodus of viewers away from the BBC and other traditional broadcasters.

While this trend is taking place across all generations, it is most acute among young people.

For children aged between four and 15, average daily minutes of broadcast TV viewing have dropped from 104 minutes in 2016 to just 36 minutes in 2024, according to data from regulator Ofcom.

Instead, YouTube is now the most-watched video service for this age group, accounting for 28pc of all viewing time.

One in five children heads straight to the YouTube app as soon as they turn the TV on.

The reasons are simple. Through platforms like YouTube, viewers are able to choose what they want to watch and when they want to watch it.

If that is appealing to adult audiences, its draw is even greater for children.

Favourites include educational videos from American channels such as Blippi and Ms Rachel.

Korea’s Pinkfong, which is celebrating the 10th anniversary of viral sensation Baby Shark, has more than 80 million subscribers.

Cocomelon, which features animations of classic nursery rhymes, has a staggering 200 million.

For older children, they are flocking to the likes of DanTDM, a YouTube gamer with 30 million subscribers.

Meanwhile, the BBC’s relevance to children is fading.

The broadcaster once made series that ran for decades across multiple generations, from Andy Pandy to ChuckleVision.

While some classics are still aired, many have disappeared altogether.

The BBC, which is subject to production quotas, remains the largest investor in children’s TV in the UK.

It commissions more than 300 hours of programming every year, resulting in 50 or more individual TV series.

But investment is in decline.

The broadcaster spent £94m on children’s TV shows last year, compared to a combined budget of around £141m for CBBC and CBeebies a decade ago.

The retreat is even more acute among commercial broadcasters.

As children spend their time elsewhere, advertisers have withdrawn and the genre has become loss-making. As a result, the BBC is effectively propping up the market.

Channel 5 continues to provide children’s programming through its Milkshake! offering. But its biggest success, Peppa Pig, is now owned by American toy giant Hasbro and is streamed on YouTube.

ITV shut down its loss-making children’s channel in 2023 after viewing slumped by almost 70pc between 2017 and 2022. By the end, its programmes were pulling in an average audience of just 4,000.

In July, Sky said it would stop commissioning original programmes on its dedicated children’s channel, relying instead on licencing shows from other producers.

Pop, a fixture of children’s TV in the UK for more than two decades, will shut down at the end of the year as its owner Narrative Entertainment said the channel was “simply not commercially viable”.

“To do kids nowadays, you have to be subsidised,” says Paul Dunthorne, the managing director of Narrative. “It’s not a commercial proposition any more.”

In a submission to MPs, ITV said the market for children’s television had changed “beyond all recognition”, adding: “The sustainable production and free-to-air broadcasting of new UK children’s content is increasingly an area of market failure.”

Across all public service broadcasters, children’s programme hours have dropped from 672 in 2014 to 380 in 2024, according to Ofcom.

Spending has fallen by 35pc in real terms over the same period from £118m to £77m.

For some parents, broadcasters’ complaints could seem odd.

The rise of streaming apps such as YouTube and Netflix mean children have more choice of what to watch than ever before.

However, for those sounding the alarm over the decline of kids’ TV in the UK, the issue at stake is a cultural one.

“Children’s TV characters play a powerful role in shaping the UK’s cultural identity by acting as mirrors of societal values, ambassadors of British creativity, and shared symbols across generations,” said Paramount, the US owner of Channel 5, in its submission.

“Characters like Paddington, Thomas & Friends and Fireman Sam reflect quintessential British traits – politeness, eccentricity, community spirit and humour.

“These characters often navigate everyday life in distinctly British settings, reinforcing cultural norms and values in a way that’s accessible to children.”

The concerns are echoed by the BBC, which warned that without intervention, “the possibility for children to see themselves and their country’s culture in stories on-screen will continue to be reduced”.

Online safety is another concern.

Campaigners including Broadcast 2040+, which is lobbying for terrestrial TV services to be maintained for longer, have warned that unregulated online platforms risk exposing children to “violent, sexual, misleading, or overly commercialised content”.

A YouTube spokesman said: “On YouTube, families access a wide range of children’s content from across the UK and beyond, opening up a range of perspectives and experiences.

“We incentivise high quality content for kids through our quality principles, which guide what’s recommended to younger viewers, and provide parents with robust controls to decide what content to make available to their children. We’ll keep investing in this critical area, guided by experts.”

Traditional channels are attempting to adapt.

The BBC and other broadcasters are placing more shows on YouTube to help reach young audiences where they are. However, it remains difficult to earn money through this method as YouTube sweeps up most of the advertising revenues.

Through initiatives such as the BBC’s TalentWorks, broadcasters are also forging partnerships with YouTube personalities in an effort to emulate the kind of programming kids most like to watch online.

TV bosses want more help. They are lobbying for regulators to go further in ensuring public service broadcasters are featured more prominently on streaming platforms such as YouTube. Ofcom this summer urged YouTube to do more to boost shows from the likes of the BBC, ITV and Channel 4.

The industry is also calling for an extension of existing film and TV tax credits to give producers an added incentive to make “culturally relevant” children’s TV shows, as well as the revival of the Young Audiences Content Fund, a government scheme to support programming for young people that closed in 2022.

Ultimately, though, the crisis in children’s TV is a consequence of a wider exodus of viewers to platforms like YouTube.

Unless the BBC and others can work out how to stop this, British children’s television could soon become a thing of the past.

“It’s just a relevance piece,” says Dunthorne. “The British have a long history of creating really amazing things, but we’re not actually commissioning anything any more because there is no market to commission it.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Cut ‘eye-watering’ net zero taxes for factories, ministers urged
data.description:
British manufacturers should be freed from paying the Government’s net zero taxes to reduce their “eye-watering” energy bills, factory owners have urged.

Make UK, which represents manufacturers, has told ministers to expand a subsidy scheme which reduces green energy taxes to tens of thousands more companies across the country.

Under the current scheme – announced by Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, in June – energy intensive companies can be exempted from paying green levies such as the Renewables Obligation and the Feed-in Tariff.

The exemption was offered to 7,000 of the UK’s most energy intensive companies – but Make UK wants the proposal, known as the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme, expanded to every one of the UK’s 115,000 manufacturers.

Make UK also warned the planned relief risks coming too late to save many companies, with the discounts not starting until 2027.

Stephen Phipson, the chief executive of Make UK, said: “The clock is ticking on tackling our eye-watering energy costs and it is now more a case of political will rather than any technical constraints to addressing these.

“It is imperative that the planned scheme is not only extended to all manufacturers but brought forward.”

The Government has claimed the programme would cut bills for some businesses by up to 25pc, but Make UK warned current plans risked leaving swathes of British industry at risk of “managed decline”.

In proposals shared with the Treasury and the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Make UK called on the Government to expand the discounts.

Make UK said the total cost of such an extension would be £3.3bn but that a wider subsidy would benefit the economy with similar levels of growth and allow more businesses to survive the energy transition.

It added that elevated carbon prices could help bring in hundreds of millions of pounds of additional revenues for the Government by 2030, which would also cover some of the cost of the programme.

Chancellor considers energy costs relief

UK manufacturers face some of the highest energy bills in the world, with electricity costing 60pc higher than most International Energy Agency members.

These punitive costs have led to a steady drum beat of warnings that Britain’s remaining manufacturing base faces a battle for survival.

In September, Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals group Ineos said it would halt all new UK investment, blaming spiralling energy bills and higher taxes.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, has reportedly been exploring a multi-billion pound package to bring down energy costs as part of her Budget on Nov 26, with bills for a typical household set to rise by £111 from April.

This has included exploring a 5pc VAT cut on domestic gas and electricity bills.

In a letter last week, more than 60 groups including Octopus Energy and Eon urged the Chancellor to prioritise bringing down electricity bills, rather than gas costs, and to remove more green energy levies.

Make UK warned that without intervention aimed at businesses, Britain will face “higher energy costs, weaker investment and the managed decline of key industries”.

Fresh data on Sunday showed business output only marginally improved last month. BDO’s Output Index, a survey of 4,000 businesses, rose to 97.49 from 96.25 in September, implying a minor increase in performance.

A government spokesman said: “We know the cost of energy is one of the greatest challenges facing industry – and this Government is acting to tackle it.

“Thousands of manufacturing businesses will see their electricity bills cut by 25pc through the British Industrial Competitiveness Scheme. We will be launching a consultation on eligibility for the scheme in the next few weeks.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Eurostar needs more competition than just Branson’s Virgin
data.description:
There will be some cabin crew in bright red uniforms. There will probably be some loud music and plenty of dry ice. And Sir Richard Branson could almost certainly show up in a rocket, a balloon or perhaps even a submarine, pulling some kind of stunt or other.

We can expect plenty of glitzy razzamatazz when the first Virgin train heads out of London to the Continent in a few years’ time, breaking Eurostar’s monopoly of rail travel through the Channel Tunnel.

And yet we should not stop there. There should be lots of operators competing on that route – not just two.

After a long struggle, Virgin last week finally won its battle to run trains to the rest of Europe after securing approval from the Office of Rail and Road.

It will still need to get safety approvals from regulators in both the UK and the European Union, negotiate platform space and work out a deal with Eurostar. But it hopes to have a service to Paris, Brussels and Amsterdam up and running by the end of the decade.

For the first time in the three decades since the tunnel linking the UK to the rest of Europe was opened, passengers will finally be able to choose which company they want to travel with.

And at least one rail route in the UK will get some much-needed competition, even while the rest of the network is steadily nationalised.

It is about time. Eurostar has, just like any monopoly, become complacent and expensive.

When I checked on Friday afternoon, if I wanted to book a trip to Paris for the following weekend, the cheapest return train ticket would cost me £260 return, and well over £300 if I decided to travel at a civilised hour, while I could get a return easyJet flight for £110.

The prices are way too high, especially given that most of the infrastructure is now three decades old. It is not as if the service is amazing either, even if it recently unveiled spiffy new gender neutral uniforms for the staff.

On Trustpilot, the train service has an average review ranking of just 2.1 out of a possible 5.0, which suggests, to put it politely, that there is a little room for improvement.

That is not nearly good enough. The entire point of the tunnel was to replace flights with train routes, both to reduce congestion at the airports and to cut carbon emissions. The train service should have driven the budget airlines out of the market on that route by now.

Instead, eye-watering ticket prices mean many of us prefer to get a cheap flight instead. As for the plans for seamless train journeys that would link into the Continent’s increasingly sophisticated high-speed rail network, and whisk us all effortlessly around France, and on to Germany, Italy or Spain, they remain on the drawing board, and have done for decades.

It is a huge missed opportunity, and one that will only start to be fixed once the tunnel is finally opened up to competition.

Here is the problem, however. Why only allow Virgin to offer an alternative service? There were three more bidders for the route who were turned down.

As well as Virgin, Gemini Trains, chaired by the Labour peer Lord Berkeley, as well as Trenalitia, the main operating arm of the Italian rail operator and the Spanish start-up Evolyn, were all interested in bidding for the route.

Instead of just one new operator, we could have had four or five. Sure, there are issues with hangar space. But even in Sir Sadiq Khan’s London, where virtually nothing gets built any more, it is surely not impossible to build some extra storage capacity for the trains if that is what is needed, and if some extra platform space is needed at St Pancras, then surely that would be possible as well.

In reality, a whole series of competitors would genuinely kickstart the market. We need a range of different services: a British Airways-style business class offering, for example, for all the people travelling to Brussels or Paris on expenses, alongside a much cheaper Ryanair-style service for people who are getting away for the weekend and would rather spend their money on dinner out than the train fare.

Sure, not all of us want to make a seven or eight-hour rail trip to Milan, or take a 10-hour through-train to Madrid, but a few people will, partly because they want to cut their carbon footprint, or perhaps because they just happen, perfectly understandably, to prefer the old-fashioned romance of the train to the cattle-car conditions of most airports.

We could even see direct routes from other British cities, perhaps with trains running from Manchester straight through to Paris, or else from Birmingham direct to Brussels.

The important point is this: we can’t know for certain which routes would be profitable or what the levels of demand might be.

But we can be sure of one thing: if we allow lots of different operators access to the rail tracks running under the English Channel, then plenty of different possible routes will be tried out, and we will find out very quickly which ones work and which ones don’t.

A duopoly is a lot better than a monopoly, but only just. It can very quickly slip back into a cosy cartel, with both companies quietly agreeing to keep prices high and to keep the competition limited. It is less hassle and more profitable.

In reality, the tunnel has been a squandered asset. It should be opened up to a whole range of different services, and it will be far better off from a blast of fierce competition between competitors.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.

data.title: Barclays accused of closing entrepreneur’s bank account over OnlyFans work
data.description:
Barclays has been accused of shutting the bank account of an entrepreneur because of her work on the adult website OnlyFans.

Madelaine Thomas, who runs a tech start-up while also making income by selling adult content, said the high street lender refused to open a new account for her business and had closed a separate account related to her work on OnlyFans and other websites.

Ms Thomas had applied for a business bank account with Barclays for her start-up, Image Angel.

The company’s technology applies digital watermarks to online images, allowing their creator to track them and identify the culprit if they are distributed or shared with others. Ms Thomas set up the company after private photos of her were distributed without her consent.

Barclays’ Eagle Labs tech programme receives government support to nurture tech start-ups, including running a “female founder accelerator”.

Ms Thomas said she had initially received positive feedback when applying for a business bank account with Image Angel, but later received a series of questions about a separate bank account that received the income from her online adult work.

She said she had also received questions about a joint account she shares with her husband, which she uses to pay nursery fees.

“It’s crazy that I’m trying to get a business bank account for technology that is going to change the landscape in terms of violence against women and girls and yet I’m now being investigated for, I don’t even know what,” she said.

“The technology I’m creating is just protecting people. It’s a benefit to society.”

Writing on LinkedIn she said: “Barclays don’t like that I earn via platforms like OnlyFans.”

OnlyFans has almost five million creators and more than 350 million users. Ms Thomas is not the only OnlyFans creator who has had their Barclays account affected. The model Jessica Alves also said her account was deleted by the bank in 2021.

Last year, the Financial Conduct Authority warned banks against denying access for adult workers.

“As adult entertainment industry representatives explained to us, these account denials or terminations could lead to significant harm for individuals running those businesses,” the regulator said at the time.

Barclays said: “We have a responsibility to understand the source of funds when banking a business, regardless of the nature of that business.

“Where it is not possible to do so, we will examine each business on a case by case basis and we will only close a customer’s account after careful thought and in line with our product terms and conditions. We do not take this decision lightly, understanding the difficulties that having banking services withdrawn can cause.”

Ms Thomas said she had been able to open a business account with another bank.

data.title: Labour’s North Sea ban is damaging business, says ExxonMobil
data.description:
Energy giant ExxonMobil has accused Labour of damaging British business by banning new oil and gas drilling in the North Sea.

On Wednesday, Paul Greenwood, Exxon’s UK chairman, said Ed Miliband’s block on new exploration licences, along with a lethal mix of carbon taxes and high energy prices, was “undermining” business and killing off North Sea energy.

It follows Exxon’s decision on Tuesday to shut down the Mossmorran chemical plant in Fife. The factory, which made a chemical called ethylene, relied heavily on ethane for production, but Labour’s energy policy had made this too expensive, Mr Greenwood said.

“Our ethylene plant and its closure is an example of how bad [government] policy is going to hurt jobs and undermine economic growth in the UK,” he said.

“We take gas from the North Sea and separate out a gas called ethane, from which our plant in Fife creates ethylene.

“It means I need cheap sources of abundant ethane, and I do not have it because North Sea output, due to government policy, is declining rapidly, and that ethane is increasingly high priced.”

His criticism was aimed at Mr Miliband’s decision to stop issuing new North Sea exploration licences, meaning oil and gas supplies are waning rapidly as existing fields become depleted. About 180 of the UK’s current 280 active fields are predicted to close by 2030.

Mr Greenwood said Exxon had spent months warning the Government of the threats to the plant – but to no avail.

“We reached out to the Government back in April and had very open and frank conversations with them. I’m pleased that somebody could spend time with newer secretaries of state and ministers explaining our challenges,” he told the BBC.

“We wanted to just be open – explaining how these policies are undermining our business.

“The Government decided at that point that they weren’t going to be able to change those policies in order to make the plant economic. That was the decision they made, and we had to live by that decision.

“What is really disappointing now, though, is that all of those policies are still in place, which means the North Sea will continue to decline.

“It means we will continue to have more imports in the UK. We will be less secure in terms of our energy supply and global emissions will increase because these products will not be made there, but will be transported in.”

He also lambasted the Government over its policy of taxing CO2 emissions, warning that this was imposing huge costs on UK manufacturers without any similar penalties on their overseas competitors.

“These are deliberate government policies that are undermining us,” he added.

Exxon’s closure coincides with a report from manufacturers’ trade body Make UK warning that surging energy costs and taxes threaten Britain’s industrial base.

Elizabeth de Jong, the chief executive of Fuels Industry UK, which represents refining businesses, also said the closure was “another stark reminder of how fragile the UK’s industrial base has become”.

Chris McDonald, the industry minister, said the Government had rejected the idea of financial support for Exxon’s plant because of the cost.

“I want to stress that, as disappointing and concerning as the closure of the Mossmorran site is, it is not symptomatic of UK industry as a whole. Our commitment to supporting UK industry is clear.”

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero was asked for a response.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Energy debts ‘to hit £6bn’ as a quarter of homes struggle to pay bills
data.description:
Household energy debts are poised to hit £6bn next year, a new industry study has warned.

A quarter of UK households will become unable to pay their gas and electricity bills in 2026, according to the latest forecast from Baringa, an energy consultancy.

Debts have already surged from £2bn three years ago to £4.4bn this summer but the study suggests looming price rises will leave millions of households struggling this winter.

Baringa, which has access to the debt books of major suppliers, suggests debts will jump towards £6bn in 2026 as arrears accelerate.

The findings will amplify the pressure on Rachel Reeves to address energy costs in the Budget on Nov 26, with likely demands for a review of net zero levies and other non-energy costs that comprise nearly two thirds of typical energy bills.

The crisis is also putting pressure on energy companies, with the scale of non-payment now putting pressure on balance sheets. The forecasts come shortly after the collapse of supplier Tomato Energy and as Ovo, Britain’s fourth biggest energy company, launches a steep cost-cutting push to repair it finances.

James Cooper, a partner at Baringa, said energy debt levels had become unsustainable, adding: “A significant proportion of this debt is unlikely to ever be recovered. 70pc of debt is unsecured, and over half is more than 12 months old.

“Financial vulnerability continues to rise, with 6.9m of households likely be financially vulnerable this winter, and 2.3m of these households in extreme vulnerability.

“In the absence of any meaningful solutions ... industry debt is forecast to increase towards £6bn in 2026.”

Baringa’s forecasts suggest that around a quarter of Britain’s 28.6 million households will struggle to pay their bills.

The UK has some of the world’s highest energy costs after a sharp rise in bills from the energy crisis of 2022 onwards. Average bills rose from £1,179 in the autumn of 2019 to £1,755 today.

While the initial surge was driven by a spike in gas prices, costs are now being kept high mainly by the 11 or so levies and other policy costs used to fund the expansion of renewables.

Baringa said the debt surge would hit already-poor households hardest, including single parents and young families.

Both Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, and Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, have expressed alarm at forecasts suggesting that household bills will rise by at least £100 next April following a £35 increase last month.

Josh Buckland, of EDF, which supplies four million UK customers, said there was zero chance of bills declining without government intervention.

He said: “On our projection, bills are likely to be around 12pc higher in 2030 than they are today. This would leave bills 54pc above pre-crisis 2021 levels.”

Ms Reeves has floated a raft of ideas on how to lower bills, including cutting the 5pc VAT on power and gas and reducing funding for heat pumps and home insulation to redirect the money.

Rapidly rising debts are not just an issue for homeowners. Suppliers are increasingly worried that unpaid bills will damage their cash flow and spark a backlash among paying customers who face being billed for the missing money.

Raising the alarm

Ned Hammond, of Energy UK, the trade body for energy suppliers, said its members had been raising the alarm about rising customer debt for some time now.

“Those in debt on average owe their supplier more than £2,000. Not only does this mean they are struggling to heat their homes, but with so many unpaid bills, suppliers face considerable challenges recovering their costs.

Octopus Energy said that without reforms to the energy market, debts would keep increasing with disastrous consequences. A spokesman said: “Ultimately, it’s up to the Government and regulator to step up with market reforms that make bills affordable again.”

Peter Smith, of National Energy Action, which campaigns on behalf of poorer households, said: “Spiralling energy debt causes unbearable stress and anxiety in many households, as well as driving up energy prices for other consumers and reducing economic activity through lower disposable income.

“It’s time for the UK Government to act decisively and meet their commitments to directly reduce bills.”

Ofgem, the energy market regulator, said it was working on debt relief schemes but acknowledged it remained “a challenge that requires urgent action”.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “We are urgently working with Ofgem to drive out debt from the energy system,” but did not specify how this might be achieved.

data.title: Labour plots blocks of flats near green belt train stations
data.description:
Labour is plotting a green belt building blitz with plans to make it far easier to build blocks of flats near commuter stations.

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, is preparing to grant default approval for building projects near well-connected train stations.

The new rules will extend to land within the green belt and include minimum housing density standards, implying properties must contain multiple dwellings.

The housing department said this was intended to “make the most of sustainable growth opportunities for local housing, jobs and businesses”.

Councils will be forced to inform the Government if they intend to block a project delivering 150 homes or more, giving ministers the opportunity to step in and overrule the decision.

As part of the plans, ministers are also preparing to scrap rules that require Sport England, The Gardens Trust and Theatres Trust to be consulted on construction projects.

Mr Reed said: “We’re making it easier to build well-connected and high-quality homes, using stronger powers to speed things up if councils drag their feet, and proposing to streamline the consultation process to cut back delays.”

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, said: “We’re ending years of dither and delay by green lighting affordable new homes for working people.”

‘Deeply concerning’

However, the proposals sparked an immediate backlash from countryside and heritage groups.

John Watkins, chair of The Gardens Trust, said: “Precious parks and gardens, which have both grown and developed over many centuries, would be lost to communities both now and in future.

“It is also incredibly frustrating and disappointing that after intimating that the launch of this consultation was imminent back in February, the Government has delayed, delayed and delayed again – creating a huge amount of uncertainty and distraction.”

Mr Watkins said the trust would “robustly contest the proposal”.

Becky Pullinger, head of land use planning at The Wildlife Trusts, called the plans “deeply concerning”.

“Whilst we must seek opportunities to build homes that integrate with sustainable transport and existing infrastructure, local authorities must be enabled to do this – not ordered to do so, regardless of local circumstance,” she said.

“This erosion of local democracy will not only strip away communities’ ability to have a say in the types of homes and infrastructure they need, but also threatens to override the safeguards in place which protect the wildlife, nature and greenspaces local residents know and love.”

Alice Roberts of CPRE, the countryside charity, said the decision was “disastrous” for traffic congestion, adding that it contradicted the Government’s net zero ambitions.

She said: “Evidence shows the vast majority of trips in such developments will be made by car as the train only goes in a specific direction and stops only at specific locations.

“It is hypocritical for the Government to say it wants ‘sustainable’ development and then green light car-dependent development in this way.”

David Simmonds MP, shadow housing minister, said: “It’s clear Britain needs more homes. But instead of trying to work with local communities to deliver homes people actually want, Labour are trying to railroad through unpopular developments.”

The new proposals come as Mr Reed strives to meet Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of parliament. Housebuilders have said they do not believe the Government will meet the target.

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: Labour treating travel industry ‘as a cash cow’, complains Jet2 boss
data.description:
Labour ministers are treating airlines and package tour operators like a “cash cow” to fund public spending, the head of Britain’s largest holiday company has claimed. 

Steve Heapy, boss of Jet2, said the Government had already swamped the sector with taxes and warned Rachel Reeves that further increases will lead to higher airfares, making it harder for cash-strapped Britons to afford their summer holidays.

“I would ask the Government not to continue using the airline and holiday industry as a cash cow and a bottomless pit of money,” Mr Heapy said.

“Any additional taxation on the airline and holiday industry is inevitably passed on to customers and that will put prices up.”

Mr Heapy said that the Chancellor, who is believed to be considering further increases to the air passenger duty (APD) departure tax, risked making it too expensive for the poorest Britons to take a break at all.

“You can’t escape the fundamental laws of economics and it could be that increased prices result in reduced demand. That’s not good, because the people who will be unable to afford a holiday will be the lowest-earning members of society,” he said.

“That would be a perverse outcome, that flying would become something for the rich and privileged.”

He said that for many Jet2 customers, who he said “work 50 or 51 weeks of the year and treasure their holiday”, a week away is the most important annual purchase.

Increases in APD announced in Ms Reeves’s first Budget will come into effect in April and include a £2 increase in the tax for short-haul international flights, the first since 2012.

For longer flights, the rate will go up by £12 to a maximum of £106, while the rate levy on domestic flights – payable in both directions – will climb to £8.

Airports are also bracing for an anticipated 400pc jump in business rates following the adoption of a new method for calculating the property tax and as passenger numbers surge.

Heathrow faces a bill of about £600m, Gatwick £200m and Manchester Airports Group £150m, which they have warned will be passed to airlines and passengers. Lobby group AirportsUK has written to the Chancellor urging her to cap the increase at 40pc.

Jet2, Britain’s largest tour operator and third-biggest airline, said customers were continuing to book later in the year.

The trend reflects concerns about the cost of living, which is driving consumers to delay purchases until the last minute.

More people are also taking up offers that lock in prices for a £60 deposit, Mr Heapy said.

Flight-only fares fell 7pc over the summer as Jet2 was forced to slash prices and spend more on marketing to fill its planes. That helped lift passenger numbers 6pc to 14m in the half year, while pre-tax profit rose by 1pc to £800m.

Jet2, based at Leeds Bradford airport and with its biggest hub in Manchester, is targeting a major expansion in southern England after announcing last week that it would open a base at Gatwick next March.

It said the performance of newly opened bases in Luton and Bournemouth, its first in the south, had been “encouraging”.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: Renters’ Rights Act: What the new law means for renters and landlords
data.description:
The Renters’ Rights Bill has become the Renters’ Rights Act, after it received Royal Assent yesterday.

The Government must next set out its plans to implement the changes and bring the law into force.

Tenants who rent in the private sector will receive greater protections from eviction, unfair rent increases and greater stability of tenure.

The controversial body of legislation is the biggest change to renting in over 30 years, according to industry body The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA).

Here, Telegraph Money explains the measures laid out in the bill, what that means for landlords and when it is likely to become law.

  • Renters’ Rights Act key points
  • What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?
  • New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act
  • How will it affect landlords?
  • Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Renters’ Rights Act key points

  • End of Section 21 “no fault” evictions: instead, landlords wanting to regain possession of a rental property will need to use approved grounds
  • Rent increases can only happen once a year: any increases must reflect market rates
  • New property standards: under the new “Decent Homes Standard”, properties must be safe, of good value and good quality
  • End of fixed-term tenancies: new and existing tenancies must change to rolling agreements
  • New landlord ombudsman and database: will give tenants access to impartial and binding resolution for complaints against landlords. All landlords must sign up
  • New pet rules: tenants have the right to request pets, but landlords can stipulate pet insurance must be taken out to protect from any resulting damage
  • Rental payment overhaul: landlords cannot accept offers above the advertised asking price for rentals, and no more than one month’s rent can be paid in advance.

What was the Renters’ Rights Bill?

The Renters’ Rights Bill built on the previous Renters’ Reform Bill, introduced by the Conservatives. Now the Renters’ Rights Act, it will introduce new protections and rights for the estimated 11 million private tenants in England by reforming the current system of renting.

An end to fixed-term tenancies, longer notice periods to give renters more time to find a new home and restrictions on rent increases will give tenants more rights and landlords less control over how they manage their property and buy-to-let business.

Labour says it will “transform the experience of private renting” which will largely be achieved by ending Section 21 evictions dubbed “no-fault evictions”.

The NRLA has argued the rules could force landlords out of the sector – reinforced by a survey earlier this year by Pegasus Insight, which revealed 37pc of landlords planned to sell a property in the next 12 months while just 6pc said they intended to buy.

New rules in the Renters’ Rights Act

Evictions and serving notice:

  • Section 21 no-fault evictions will be abolished.
  • Fixed-term tenancies will be replaced with periodic tenancies, also known as rolling contracts that don’t have an end date.
  • Tenants will have a 12-month protected period at the start of their tenancy preventing landlords from forcing evictions to move back in or sell.
  • Tenants can serve two months’ notice to quit the property at any time.
  • Landlords must give four months’ notice if they wish to move back in or sell a rental property.
  • Landlords will not be able to evict a tenant in arrears until three months’ payment have been missed, up from two months while the notice period has been increased from two to four weeks.

Prices and payment:

  • Rent increases are limited to once a year and only to the market rate, which can be challenged by tenants at the first-tier tribunal if they believe it is excessive. Increases cannot be backdated.
  • Large amounts of rent cannot be demanded in advance of a tenancy starting.
  • Landlords must register with a digital database, failing to do so means that they cannot use certain grounds of possession.
  • Asking rents must be published to prevent bidding wars, making it illegal for landlords to accept offers higher than the rent advertised.

Standards and safety:

  • The Decent Homes Standard and Awaab’s Law, currently applicable to homes in the social housing sector, will be extended to private sector landlords to ensure homes are made safe, are of good value and good quality.
  • A private rented sector landlord ombudsman will be set up to provide an impartial and binding resolution for tenant complaints against landlords.

Discrimination:

  • Strengthened rights for tenants to have pets, although landlords can insist on pet insurance to cover any damage to their property.
  • End discrimination against tenants on benefits or with children.

Compliance:

  • Councils will have the power to issue civil penalties of up to £7,000 to landlords who don’t comply with the reforms, rising to £40,000 for a serious breach or repeat non-compliance with the law.
  • More strength will be given to rent repayment orders that can be issued by councils when landlords breach the law.

How will it affect tenants?

The Renters’ Rights Act will give tenants greater protections and stronger rights when they’re renting.

As outlined in the rules above, among the changes the new law will usher in, tenants must be given longer notice periods when their landlords want to move back in or sell the property they’re renting, the property must legally reach the Decent Homes Standard, and they will no longer be subject to frequent rent increases.

For any tenants with a complaint about their landlord, the introduction of an independent ombudsman will help readdress the natural power dynamic and help tenants to reach a resolution.

How will it affect landlords?

Sylvie Harris, of property consultancy INHOUS, said: “Through the abolition of Section 21 no-fault evictions, landlords will only be able to end tenancies using specific grounds under Section 8, such as rent arrears or anti-social behaviour or if they intend to sell or move into the property.”

For the latter, an extended four-month notice period must be served.

But the pressure this will place on a court system, which Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, acknowledged during a parliamentary debate was “on its knees”, is raising concerns and has led to an amendment being tabled to review the impact of this plan on the legal system.

When the Conservative government brought forward its draft renters rights legislation, then-minister Michael Gove said that the right to make no-fault evictions would remain in place until a full review of the courts had been completed.

Currently, the average time between a landlord submitting a claim for an eviction and regaining possession of their property is more than seven months. In response to the Government’s claims that the courts will be ready for the impact of the changes, Ben Beadle, chief executive of the NRLA, said: “Seven months is an eternity for responsible landlords who may be dealing with serious rent arrears and for neighbours having to endure anti-social behaviour.”

Not only could it take longer to evict a tenant, landlords will have to endure greater levels of arrears before they apply for possession as the minimum level of arrears for eviction is bumped up from two to three months.

Landlords operating in the student let sector could also face challenges in operating their businesses efficiently if the legislation is implemented in its current form.

Ending fixed-term tenancies removes the certainty for these landlords that their property will be available to let to a new batch of students when the academic year begins.

Although grounds for possession have been included, which allows landlords of houses in multiple occupation (HMOs) to evict last year’s students to make way for a new group, one and two-bedroom student flats were not mentioned.

Banning the receipt of large sums of rent upfront, meanwhile, could put landlords off renting to international students or tenants who have had credit difficulties in the past or do not pass affordability assessments. Without the security of payment in advance, these groups may struggle to be accepted.

When will the bill become law?

The Government has announced May 1 2026 as the date when Section 21 evictions will be outlawed in England. Also from this date, fixed-term tenancy contracts must be replaced by ‘rolling’ contracts, there’ll be an end to bidding wars and landlords must include clearer rules on pets.

Will it apply to existing tenancies?

Yes, it will. We are still waiting to find out the finer details, but the plan is to make the changes to tenancies in one go rather than create a two-tier system that applies the rules to new and existing tenancies at different times.

data.title: British Airways owner threatens to shift investment to Madrid amid Heathrow row
data.description:
The chief executive of British Airways (BA) has warned that its Spanish owner could ditch investment in Heathrow if the bill for the airport’s third runway is too high.

Sean Doyle said International Airlines Group (IAG) is concerned by the cost of Heathrow’s £49bn expansion, which will be largely paid for by carriers and passengers.

He said IAG does not support plans to move the M25 to accommodate the expansion, while he also urged officials to consider pursuing a shorter, cheaper runway.

A failure to keep passenger fees under control risks making Heathrow less attractive, Mr Doyle said, while it could also lead to IAG prioritising investment in its airlines overseas, including Spain’s Iberia and Ireland’s Aer Lingus.

He said: “We’re part of a group and the group allocates capital on the basis of performance.

“If Heathrow becomes uncompetitive and that impacts the performance of an airline like BA, there’ll be opportunities to put that capital elsewhere.”

Mr Doyle added: “Madrid is flying at the minute, with Iberia reporting very strong results, and Barcelona is an expanding market we have a very strong presence in. Dublin has built a new runway for a couple of billion, so there’s headroom there.

“There will be debates about competitiveness and airport charges when it comes to the way IAG allocates capital. We’re not at that point yet but that’s why, for everybody’s sake, a competitive Heathrow is very important.”

British Airways stepped up its campaign against escalating costs at Heathrow as the Government prepares to choose between competing runway plans by the end of the month.

Heathrow Airport’s own proposals call for the construction of a 3.5km runway built over the M25, lifting annual passenger numbers from 82 million to 150 million.

A rival plan from Surinder Arora, the hotel tycoon, envisages a 2,800m strip that would be unable to take the very biggest jets when fully laden.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have put airport expansion at the heart of Labour’s bid to kickstart the economy, arguing that the pursuit of growth “trumps” net zero commitments.

Labour is seeking to speed up legal processes in a bid to secure planning permission for a third runway before the next general election.

Mr Doyle said BA accepts that Heathrow is full but questioned whether the airport’s own model is unnecessarily complex.

He said: “We need to make sure we’re competitive and build this runway as quickly and efficiently as we can, and any opportunity to achieve that goal should be explored.

“If you can avoid moving the M25, you should avoid moving the M25. You can’t argue against the logic of that. And I think we should look at ways of potentially building a shorter runway.”

‘Serious calculations’

Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, hit back at the claims, dismissing Mr Doyle’s suggestion that building over the motorway may be unnecessary.

He said: “I agree with him – if we could avoid it, we should – but it is not avoidable. It’s expensive but it’s a well-controlled, known technology solution.

“If you build a shorter runway, you have to move more motorways, like the M4 spur, which is more complicated. It’s in several layers with a railway underneath and you have nowhere to put your motorways or your building site and soil.”

He said Mr Arora’s plan would require demolition of more than 2,000 homes, compared with only about 750 for Heathrow’s own proposals, and that the difference had not been costed.

Mr Woldbye said that could make a shorter runway more expensive and that “serious calculations” indicated that only a full-length runway would keep longer-term costs to a minimum.

He said: “You’d end up with much less capacity. That does not make sense. If you want lower fares, you need the maximum number of passengers to spread costs.”

The chief executive said that Heathrow would nevertheless look at any decision from Labour recommending a cheaper alternative to its blueprint.

Asked if the airport could guarantee that costs for expansion would be capped at £49bn, Mr Woldbye said that “naturally I cannot guarantee that” since the runway would not be ready for a decade and a contingency of 20-30pc “is normal”.

He added: “We have no reason to be more expensive than we should be. We are a regulated entity, our interest is to be as efficient as we possibly can.”

data.title: Ocado suffers £300m slump as US supermarket axes its warehouses
data.description:
Ocado has suffered a £300m slump after its biggest customer announced plans to shut automated warehouses built using its technology.

Kroger, a US supermarket chain, said it would close three facilities set up with Ocado as it shifts towards fulfilling orders in-store. The sites, known as customer fulfilment centres, are expected to cease operations in January.

Ocado said shutting down the warehouses in Maryland, Wisconsin and Florida would deliver a $50m (£38m) blow to revenues next year.

Shares in the company plunged by as much as 16.5pc following the announcement, wiping nearly £300m off Ocado’s market value.

Kroger’s decision casts doubt over Ocado’s ambitions to become the “Tesla of groceries”, supplying smart warehouse technology to supermarkets around the world. Clive Black, at Shore Capital, called it “a near knock-out punch”.

Ocado’s shares have fallen by 38pc since Kroger first cast doubt over the future of a tie-up with the company in September.

Once best known as an online grocer, Ocado has in recent years pivoted to selling its automated warehouse technology to supermarkets around the world and works with Morrisons in the UK.

It first partnered with Kroger in 2018. The deal helped power Ocado to the FTSE 100, thanks to Kroger’s scale. Its colossal retail empire spans more than 2,700 stores across 35 American states.

Kroger has since built eight warehouses with Ocado, but said in September it was “taking a hard look” at the technology, casting doubt on plans to build 20 facilities.

The closure of three of its eight warehouses will cost Kroger $2.6bn, the company said. However, the supermarket’s shares rose 1.8pc in early trading on Wall Street after it vowed to examine partnerships with delivery companies as part of an altered digital strategy.

Warehouses ‘do not work economically’

Competitors have switched to fulfilling online orders from shops rather than warehouses, mimicking a model used by Morrisons, Co-op and Iceland in the UK under a partnership with Amazon, the owner of Deliveroo.

Mr Black of Shore Capital said most of Ocado’s warehouses “do not work economically in the USA or the mass-market first world, in truth”.

“They may work in dense and affluent urban locations, but that means Ocado’s previously spoken total addressable market (TAM) has been blitzed,” he said.

He added this was “a smelling-salt moment for the group, and we worry further about the real value of what is left in this firm”.

Kroger’s change in approach follows the abrupt departure of chief executive Rodney McMullen in March, a longstanding ally of Tim Steiner, Ocado’s founder.

His interim successor, chairman Ron Sargent, said Kroger was increasingly fulfilling orders in-store and as a result was conducting a “site-by-site” review of its automated fulfilment network.

Ocado said it would still operate its remaining Kroger warehouses in Ohio, Texas, Georgia, Colorado and Michigan.

It said it “continues to engage with Kroger on these and other matters and expects significant growth in the US market”.

Kroger will pay Ocado more than $250m in compensation to shut down the sites.

Still, the cancellation of the contract is a heavy blow for a company that suffered pre-tax losses of £375m last year on turnover of £1.2bn.

Sarah Roberts, an analyst at Barclays, acknowledged earlier this month that any decision to close warehouses would “materially impact” earnings and “would also likely weigh on sentiment around Ocado’s long-term customer fulfilment centres’ viability”.

Brittain Ladd, a former Amazon executive and consultant who advised Kroger on its deal with Ocado, told The Telegraph earlier this year: “If Ocado loses the account with Kroger or the partnership is scaled back, it’s unlikely Ocado will sign any other customers in the US.”

data.title: HP seeks £1.5bn from Mike Lynch’s estate
data.description:
Hewlett Packard (HP) is seeking £1.5bn from the estate of the late British tech tycoon Mike Lynch in a claim likely to wipe out his fortune.

The Silicon Valley company has argued it is owed the sum after winning a long-running fraud case over its purchase of Lynch’s tech company Autonomy in 2011.

The claim, made in legal filings on Tuesday, is substantially more than the £740m in damages that a judge said HP had suffered as a result of Lynch inflating Autonomy’s value.

If successful, it would dwarf the size of Lynch’s fortune, which has been estimated at around £500m, and would bankrupt his estate.

While the Lynch estate would face being wiped out by an adverse ruling, many of the family’s assets are held in his wife’s, Angela Bacares, name. These would not be affected by any potential ruling.

Lynch, once described as “Britain’s Bill Gates”, sold Autonomy to Hewlett Packard for £7bn in 2011. The deal made him hundreds of millions but soon turned sour with HP accusing him of fraud.

HP sued Lynch at the High Court in 2015 and he was found liable for fraud in 2022.

Lynch was cleared of US criminal charges last year over the deal, but he and his teenage daughter Hannah were among seven who died while celebrating his freedom on his Bayesian superyacht shortly after the trial concluded.

His estate is now battling HP over damages owed to the US company and is seeking permission to appeal the fraud ruling.

Mr Justice Hildyard said in July that HP had suffered damages of around £740m as a result of Lynch and former Autonomy finance chief Sushovan Hussain inflating the company’s value with a series of accounting tricks.

A final award is due to be determined based on interest due to HP since the deal was completed, as well as legal costs and foreign exchange conversions.

HP claims that it is owed more than $760m (£578m) in interest, as well as almost £120m in legal fees.

Lynch’s estate, which is being managed by Jeremy Sandelson, a Clifford Chance lawyer, is disputing the company’s figures.

The trial that led to the judgment was one of the longest in English legal history. Mr Hussain settled with HP separately for £78m earlier this year.

Lynch’s estate has sought permission to challenge both the estimation of the damages and the finding that the vehicle HP used to acquire Autonomy was deceived.

A spokesman for the Lynch family said: “Today’s hearing addresses technical matters that change nothing about the underlying substance of the case. The core facts remain that HP’s claim was fundamentally flawed and a wild overstatement.

“By any account, including the court’s, HP’s own mismanagement destroyed the vast majority of Autonomy’s value. Dr Lynch’s acquittal in the US criminal trial exposed the truth when witnesses were properly cross-examined.”

HP said: “We are pleased that this hearing brings us a step closer to the resolution of this dispute.”

data.title: Medics’ strike threatens to shut down North Sea oil platforms
data.description:
Striking medics are threatening to shut down North Sea oil and gas platforms belonging to the UK’s biggest offshore producer in a dispute over pay.

Around a dozen medics who work on platforms owned by Harbour Energy are poised to down tools in three 24-hour stoppages, with the first starting on Friday Nov 21.

Offshore platforms are legally obliged to have medical cover for staff in case of accidents or illness so if the medics withdraw next week as planned it could cut oil output worth tens of millions of pounds.

The dispute is not with Harbour itself – the medics are all employed by MCL Medics, a contractor providing occupational health cover for offshore companies.

Unite the union, which is representing the medics, said the industrial action was driven by a long-running battle with MCL over pay levels and training allowances.

Sharon Graham, Unite general secretary, said: “Offshore medics are a vital lifesaving asset for workers on Harbour Energy installations.”

Ms Graham added: “They absolutely deserve to be properly rewarded for their specialised and highly skilled role in the offshore industry. The MCL medics will have Unite’s unequivocal support in the fight to secure better jobs, pay and conditions.”

Harbour is a major operator in the North Sea and also has platforms in Norway, Argentina and Mexico. It produces the equivalent of 156,000 barrels of oil per day.

John Boland, a Unite industrial officer, said: “This has been a long and protracted dispute because MCL Medics totally refuse to reward the workers with the pay rise they deserve. Let’s remember offshore platforms simply can’t operate without the medics.”

The 13 medics are to walk out for 24 hours on Nov 21, Dec 5 and Dec 19. A continuous ban on overtime will also start on Nov 21.

The sites affected are Harbour’s Armada, Britannia, Jasmine, Judy, Lomond and North Everest platforms.

‘Robust contingency plans’

Rules set out by the Health and Safety Executive, which oversees safety, say that offshore platforms must have first aid facilities and trained staff at all times. In practice this means that when there are 25 or more workers on an offshore installation then a medic must be on hand.

Medics do not always have to be doctors – they can also include registered nurses or paramedics, who hold an approved Offshore Medic Certificate. However, the first aid arrangements for any installation must be approved by a doctor.

A Harbour spokesman said it planned to keep its platforms in operation, implying it would bring replacement medics on to platforms.

“We are in contact with MCL Medics as they continue to engage with their employees on this matter. We hope that all parties can find a resolution.

“In the meantime, the safety of our people remains our top priority and we are working to ensure there is no impact to our operations.”

According to trade journal Energy Voice, MCL claimed to have “robust contingency plans in place to maintain safe, uninterrupted services across all sites”.

MCL Medics was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph
